questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents for annual screening. According to current US guidelines, which of the following is the recommended screening modality for this patient?

choices.label: E
choices.text: No screening recommended for this patient

explanation.summary: US guidelines recommend annual low-dose spiral CT (LDCT) screening for high-risk patients: age 50-80 with â‰¥20 pack-year smoking history who currently smoke or quit within the past 15 years. LDCT reduces lung cancer death risk by 20%.
explanation.whyCorrect: Current US screening recommendations include annual low-dose spiral CT scan (LDCT) for high-risk patients aged 50-80 with a â‰¥20 pack-year smoking history who are current smokers or quit within the past 15 years. This 65-year-old with a 40-pack-year history meets all criteria. LDCT screening reduces relative risk of death from lung cancer by 20%.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chest X-ray is not the recommended screening tool. LDCT is more sensitive for detecting early lung cancers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sputum cytology is not recommended for lung cancer screening. LDCT is the standard.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PET scans are used for staging confirmed cancers, not for routine screening.
whyWrongByChoice.E: This patient meets all criteria for screening (age 50-80, â‰¥20 pack-years, current smoker). Screening IS recommended.

questions.stem: Primary lung cancers are broadly divided into two main categories based on differences in metastasis rates and treatment approaches. Which of the following correctly describes these categories and their relative frequencies?

explanation.summary: Primary lung cancers are divided into non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC, 85%) and small cell lung cancer (SCLC, 15%). SCLC is aggressive and often metastasized at diagnosis; NSCLC is less aggressive and may be surgically resectable.
explanation.whyCorrect: Primary lung cancers are broadly divided into non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC, 85% of cases) and small cell lung cancer (SCLC, 15%). This classification is clinically critical because SCLC is highly aggressive and often already metastasized at diagnosis, while NSCLC is less aggressive and, if confined to the lung, can sometimes be managed with surgery.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenocarcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma are subtypes of NSCLC, not the broad classification. The primary division is NSCLC vs. SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This reverses the frequencies. NSCLC is more common (85%), not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carcinoid tumors and neuroendocrine tumors are less common subtypes, not the major classification.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mesothelioma is not a lung cancerâ€”it is a cancer of the pleural lining surrounding the lung.

questions.stem: A patient with lung cancer develops facial and hand swelling that worsens when lying down, along with headache and dyspnea. Which of the following complications is most likely?

explanation.summary: SVC syndrome occurs when a tumor compresses the SVC, reducing blood flow from the head and upper extremities. It presents with facial and hand swelling, headache worsening with recumbency, and dyspnea. Cancer is the most common cause (60%).
explanation.whyCorrect: Superior vena cava (SVC) syndrome occurs when a tumor compresses the SVC, reducing blood flow from the head and upper extremities. It presents with swelling of the face and hands, a feeling of 'head fullness,' headache worsened by lying down, and dyspnea. Cancer is the most common cause (60% of cases), especially lung cancer and mediastinal lymphoma.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Horner syndrome (miosis, partial ptosis, anhidrosis) is caused by tumor compression of the sympathetic trunk, not SVC compression. It presents with eye and facial findings, not facial/hand swelling.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pancoast tumor causes neuropathic arm pain from brachial plexus compression by an apical lung tumor, not facial/hand swelling.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lambert-Eaton syndrome is a paraneoplastic neurologic syndrome causing proximal muscle weakness, not SVC obstruction symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.E: SIADH causes hyponatremia with fatigue and confusion but does not cause the facial/hand swelling pattern of SVC syndrome.

questions.stem: A patient with small cell lung cancer has persistent hyponatremia. Which of the following paraneoplastic syndromes is most likely responsible?

explanation.summary: SCLC can produce ectopic ADH (vasopressin), causing SIADH with increased renal water reabsorption and dilutional hyponatremia. Symptoms include fatigue, nausea, confusion, and seizures.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tumor secretion of ADH (vasopressin) by SCLC causes the syndrome of inappropriate ADH secretion (SIADH), leading to increased renal water reabsorption and hyponatremia. Symptoms of hyponatremia include fatigue, nausea, confusion, and seizures. SIADH is one of the most common paraneoplastic syndromes in SCLC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: PTHrP secretion causes hypercalcemia (not hyponatremia) and is most commonly seen in squamous cell carcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ectopic ACTH secretion can occur in SCLC but causes Cushingoid features (truncal obesity, weakness), not primarily hyponatremia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lambert-Eaton is a neurologic paraneoplastic syndrome causing proximal muscle weakness, not hyponatremia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hypercoagulability causes thromboembolic disease but does not directly cause hyponatremia.

questions.stem: Among the subtypes of NSCLC, which is the most common, accounting for approximately 40% of all primary lung cancers?

explanation.summary: Adenocarcinoma is the most common subtype of NSCLC and the most common primary lung cancer overall, accounting for approximately 40% of all cases. Squamous cell carcinoma accounts for ~20%, and large cell carcinoma ~3%.
explanation.whyCorrect: Adenocarcinoma is the most common type of lung cancer, accounting for approximately 40% of all primary lung cancers. It is a subtype of NSCLC. Squamous cell carcinoma is the second most common NSCLC subtype (~20%), followed by large cell carcinoma (~3%).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Squamous cell carcinoma accounts for approximately 20% of lung cancers, making it less common than adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Large cell carcinoma accounts for only about 3% of lung cancers.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carcinoid tumors are uncommon primary lung neoplasms, not in the top 3 subtypes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Adenosquamous carcinoma is a rare subtype of lung cancer.

questions.stem: Gain-of-function mutations in EGFR and ALK are most commonly found in which type of lung cancer?

explanation.summary: Gain-of-function mutations in tyrosine kinase genes like EGFR and ALK are characteristic of adenocarcinoma. These mutations are clinically important because they guide targeted therapy with EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors and ALK inhibitors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Gain-of-function mutations in tyrosine kinase genes like EGFR and ALK, as well as serine/threonine kinases like BRAF and PIK3, are commonly found in adenocarcinoma. Identifying these mutations guides targeted therapyâ€”EGFR mutations â†’ EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors; BRAF mutations â†’ BRAF inhibitors. MYC amplification is more associated with SCLC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SCLC is more associated with MYC oncogene amplification and TP53 loss. EGFR and ALK mutations are characteristic of adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Squamous cell carcinoma has its own molecular profile but EGFR and ALK mutations are more specifically associated with adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Large cell carcinoma is a less common subtype without the specific EGFR/ALK mutation association of adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mesothelioma is not a lung cancer; it is a cancer of the pleural lining. It does not have the EGFR/ALK mutations of lung adenocarcinoma.

questions.stem: Horner syndrome in a lung cancer patient presents with which of the following triads?

explanation.summary: Horner syndrome (miosis, partial ptosis, anhidrosis) results from tumor compression of the sympathetic trunk, seen mostly in NSCLC. The sympathetic chain runs along the lung apex, making apical tumors (Pancoast tumors) a common cause.
explanation.whyCorrect: Horner syndrome results from tumor compression of the sympathetic trunk and presents with the triad of miosis (constricted pupil), partial ptosis (drooping eyelid), and anhidrosis (decreased sweating) on the affected side. It is seen mostly in NSCLC, particularly apical (Pancoast) tumors that invade the sympathetic chain.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Headache, papilledema, and vomiting suggest increased intracranial pressure, possibly from brain metastasis, not Horner syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Facial swelling, dyspnea, and headache describe SVC syndrome, not Horner syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Arm pain, hand weakness, and atrophy describe Pancoast tumor with brachial plexus invasion, which can coexist with Horner syndrome but is a separate entity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Proximal weakness, fatigue, and dry mouth describe Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome, a paraneoplastic condition, not Horner syndrome.

questions.stem: On imaging, how do primary lung cancers typically differ from secondary (metastatic) lung tumors?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Primary: multiple lower lobe lesions; Metastatic: single upper lobe nodule

choices.label: B
choices.text: Primary: single upper lobe nodule; Metastatic: multiple lower lobe lesions

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both present as bilateral hilar masses

choices.label: D
choices.text: Primary: bilateral ground-glass opacities; Metastatic: unilateral consolidation

explanation.summary: Primary lung tumors are typically singular nodules located in upper lobes. Secondary (metastatic) tumors more often appear as multiple lesions in the lower lobes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Radiologically, primary lung tumors are typically singular nodules located in the upper lobes, while secondary metastatic tumors more often appear as multiple lesions in the lower lobes. This distinction, combined with the presence of cancer elsewhere in the body, helps differentiate primary from metastatic disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This reverses the typical patterns. Primary tumors are singular/upper lobe; metastatic tumors are multiple/lower lobe.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bilateral hilar masses are more associated with lymphoma or sarcoidosis, not the typical distinction between primary and metastatic lung cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ground-glass opacities are more associated with infections or inflammation. The primary vs. metastatic distinction is based on nodule number and location.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While overlap exists, the typical radiologic patterns do differ between primary and metastatic lung cancer.

questions.stem: Approximately what percentage of lung cancer cases are attributed to cigarette smoking?

explanation.summary: Approximately 85-90% of lung cancer cases are attributed to smoking. However, 10% occur in nonsmokers, many likely from secondhand smoke exposure. Smoking tobacco is the strongest predictor of lung cancer.
explanation.whyCorrect: Approximately 85-90% of lung cancer cases are attributed to smoking tobacco cigarettes. Smoking is the strongest predictor, with approximately 11% of heavy smokers eventually diagnosed with lung cancer. However, 10% of cases occur in nonsmokers, many likely from secondhand smoke exposure, so lung cancer should not be ruled out in nonsmokers.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 20-30% significantly underestimates smoking's contribution to lung cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 40-50% underestimates the proportion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 60-70% is still below the actual 85-90% attribution.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While smoking is the dominant cause, 10% of cases occur in nonsmokers, so it is not 99-100%.

questions.stem: Hypercalcemia as a paraneoplastic syndrome in lung cancer is most commonly caused by tumor secretion of PTHrP. Which histologic subtype is most associated with this finding?

explanation.summary: PTHrP secretion causing hypercalcemia is most commonly associated with squamous cell carcinoma of the lung. PTHrP mimics PTH, promoting calcium absorption from bone and the GI tract.
explanation.whyCorrect: PTHrP (parathyroid hormone-related peptide) secretion is most commonly seen in squamous cell carcinoma (the most common association). It mimics parathyroid hormone, promoting excessive calcium absorption from bone and the GI tract, causing hypercalcemia that presents with frequent urination, thirst, nausea, confusion, and potentially coma. PTHrP can also be seen in adenocarcinoma and SCLC but is classically associated with squamous cell.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SCLC is more classically associated with SIADH (hyponatremia) and ectopic ACTH. While PTHrP can occur, squamous cell is the most common association.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Large cell carcinoma is uncommon (3%) and is not the classic association for PTHrP-mediated hypercalcemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carcinoid tumors can cause carcinoid syndrome but are not classically associated with PTHrP secretion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Adenosquamous carcinoma is rare and not the classic association for PTHrP.

questions.stem: Biopsy is essential for confirming lung cancer diagnosis and guiding therapy. Which of the following methods can be used to obtain lung tissue for diagnosis?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bronchoscopy, needle biopsy, or surgical resection

explanation.summary: Lung tissue for diagnosis can be obtained via bronchoscopy, mediastinoscopy, percutaneous needle biopsy, or open surgical resection. Tissue analysis confirms cancer type and identifies genetic variants to guide targeted therapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Obtaining lung tissue is essential both for making the cancer diagnosis and for analyzing genetic variants that may guide therapy. Tissue samples are obtained by bronchoscopy, mediastinoscopy, percutaneous needle biopsy, or open surgical resection. Imaging alone cannot confirm the tissue diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While sputum cytology may sometimes detect malignant cells, it is not sufficient for definitive diagnosis and genetic analysis. Tissue biopsy is required.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Blood tumor markers may support staging but cannot confirm the histologic diagnosis or identify actionable mutations.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CT scanning is an imaging modality used for detection and staging but cannot provide tissue for histologic diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PET scans detect metabolically active lesions and aid staging but do not provide tissue for diagnosis.

questions.stem: A lung cancer patient develops neuropathic pain radiating down the arm due to tumor compression of the brachial plexus. Which of the following best describes this presentation?

explanation.summary: A Pancoast tumor is a lung tumor (usually apical NSCLC) that compresses the brachial plexus, causing neuropathic pain radiating down the arm. It may coexist with Horner syndrome if the sympathetic trunk is also involved.
explanation.whyCorrect: A Pancoast tumor is a lung tumor at the apex that invades the brachial plexus, causing neuropathic pain radiating down the arm. It may also compress the sympathetic trunk, causing concurrent Horner syndrome (miosis, ptosis, anhidrosis). Pancoast tumors are most commonly NSCLC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Horner syndrome (miosis, ptosis, anhidrosis) results from sympathetic trunk compression. While it can coexist with Pancoast tumor, the arm pain from brachial plexus compression specifically defines the Pancoast presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: SVC syndrome causes facial and upper extremity swelling, not neuropathic arm pain from nerve compression.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lambert-Eaton syndrome causes proximal muscle weakness from autoantibodies against presynaptic calcium channels, not localized neuropathic arm pain.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Paraneoplastic neuropathy is a diffuse sensory neuropathy from autoantibodies, not localized arm pain from anatomic nerve compression.

questions.stem: Which of the following features of a solitary pulmonary nodule found incidentally on chest X-ray is most suggestive of malignancy?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Nodule >2 cm in a 65-year-old smoker that is growing

explanation.summary: Features suggestive of malignancy include tobacco smoking history, older age (50% malignant in patients >60), larger size (>2 cm, malignant in >50%), and growing nodule size on repeated imaging.
explanation.whyCorrect: Clues suggesting a solitary pulmonary nodule is malignant include: tobacco smoking history, older age (50% are malignant in patients over 60, only 3% in those under 40), larger size (especially >2 cm, malignant in >50%), and growing size on serial imaging. This nodule (>2 cm, growing, in a 65-year-old smoker) has multiple high-risk features.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A small nodule in a young nonsmoker has a low probability of malignancy (only 3% of nodules in those under 40 are malignant).
whyWrongByChoice.C: Calcification (especially central or popcorn patterns) and smooth borders are features of benign nodules (granuloma, hamartoma).
whyWrongByChoice.D: A nodule stable over 2 years is more likely benign. Growth is a concerning feature for malignancy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Central calcification in a small nodule suggests a benign granuloma, not malignancy.

questions.stem: Hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy is a paraneoplastic syndrome associated with adenocarcinoma. Which of the following triads characterizes this condition?

explanation.summary: Hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy is a syndrome associated with adenocarcinoma characterized by the triad of clubbed digits, pain in the periosteum of long bones, and arthritis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy is a paraneoplastic musculoskeletal syndrome associated with adenocarcinoma of the lung. It is characterized by the triad of clubbed digits (thickening of fingertips), pain in the outer layer of long bones (periosteum), and arthritis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Miosis, ptosis, anhidrosis is Horner syndrome from sympathetic trunk compression.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Facial swelling, headache, dyspnea describes SVC syndrome from SVC compression.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypercalcemia, renal stones, bone pain describes PTHrP-mediated paraneoplastic syndrome (primarily squamous cell), not osteoarthropathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Proximal weakness, fatigue, autonomic dysfunction describes Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome.

questions.stem: Lung cancer remains the leading cause of cancer deaths despite declining incidence. Approximately how many new cases are diagnosed annually in the United States?

explanation.summary: More than 200,000 new cases of lung cancer are diagnosed each year in the United States. Despite declining incidence from smoking cessation, it remains the leading cause of cancer deaths in the US and worldwide.
explanation.whyCorrect: More than 200,000 new cases of lung cancer are diagnosed each year in the United States. Despite declining incidence due to smoking cessation programs, lung cancer persists as the leading cause of cancer deaths in the US and worldwide. Among nonskin cancers, lung cancer incidence is second only to prostate cancer in men and breast cancer in women.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 50,000 significantly underestimates annual lung cancer diagnoses. More than 200,000 cases occur annually.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 100,000 underestimates the annual incidence.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 500,000 overestimates annual new diagnoses.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 1,000,000 greatly overestimates. The US figure is over 200,000 annually.

questions.stem: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome is a neurologic paraneoplastic syndrome most commonly associated with which type of lung cancer?

explanation.summary: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome is a neurologic paraneoplastic syndrome most often seen with SCLC. It presents with proximal muscle weakness, fatigue, and bulbar symptoms, caused by autoantibodies against presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels.
explanation.whyCorrect: Neurologic paraneoplastic syndromes are seen in 3-5% of lung cancer patients, most often in those with SCLC. Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome presents as lower extremity proximal muscle weakness, fatigue, diaphragmatic weakness, and bulbar symptoms. It is caused by autoantibodies produced by the tumor that react against presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenocarcinoma is more associated with hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy and EGFR/ALK mutations.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Squamous cell carcinoma is most associated with PTHrP-mediated hypercalcemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Large cell carcinoma is uncommon and not specifically associated with Lambert-Eaton.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carcinoid tumors can cause carcinoid syndrome but not typically Lambert-Eaton.

questions.stem: Lung cancer patients are at increased risk for thromboembolic disease. Which of the following mechanisms contributes to the hypercoagulable state in lung cancer?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tumor secretion of procoagulant proteins like tissue factor

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased von Willebrand factor from tumor invasion

explanation.summary: Lung cancer induces hypercoagulability through tumor secretion of procoagulant proteins like tissue factor and cysteine proteases. This increases risk for DVT and pulmonary embolus.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hypercoagulability is common in lung cancer, induced by tumor secretion of procoagulant proteins including tissue factor and cysteine proteases. This places cancer patients at higher risk for thromboembolic diseases such as deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolus. This is a hematologic paraneoplastic syndrome.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cancer promotes coagulation (not suppresses it) through procoagulant protein secretion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cancer-induced thrombocytopenia would promote bleeding, not thrombosis. The hypercoagulable state is from procoagulant protein secretion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Von Willebrand factor is not typically decreased by tumor invasion. The hypercoagulable mechanism involves tissue factor secretion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While malnutrition can occur in cancer, vitamin K deficiency would promote bleeding, not the hypercoagulable state seen in lung cancer.

questions.stem: MYC oncogene amplification is most commonly found in which type of lung cancer?

explanation.summary: Amplification of MYC oncogenes is characteristically associated with small cell carcinoma. In contrast, EGFR and ALK mutations are more associated with adenocarcinoma, and TP53 loss occurs across multiple types.
explanation.whyCorrect: MYC oncogene amplification is characteristically found in small cell lung cancer (SCLC). MYC family genes (C-MYC, N-MYC, L-MYC) regulate cell proliferation, and their amplification drives the highly aggressive growth of SCLC. In contrast, EGFR and ALK mutations are more associated with adenocarcinoma.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenocarcinoma is associated with EGFR, ALK, BRAF, and PIK3 mutations, not MYC amplification.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Squamous cell carcinoma has its own molecular profile (TP53 mutations are common) but MYC amplification is specifically associated with SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Large cell carcinoma does not have MYC amplification as a characteristic feature.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carcinoid tumors are neuroendocrine but typically lower-grade than SCLC and not characterized by MYC amplification.

questions.stem: Asbestos exposure is a known risk factor for lung cancer. When combined with smoking, the risk is which of the following?

explanation.summary: When asbestos exposure is combined with smoking history, the risk of lung cancer is substantially increased in a multiplicative (synergistic) fashion, far exceeding the risk of either exposure alone.
explanation.whyCorrect: When asbestos exposure is combined with a smoking history, the risk of lung cancer is substantially increasedâ€”the interaction is synergistic/multiplicative rather than simply additive. This means the combined risk is far greater than the sum of individual risks. Asbestos particles lodged in the lung also migrate into the pleural sac, where they can induce mesothelioma.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The interaction is multiplicative/synergistic, not merely additive. Combined risk far exceeds the sum of individual risks.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The combined risk is much greater than smoking alone. Asbestos adds substantial additional risk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The combined risk is much greater than asbestos alone. Smoking adds enormous additional risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There is no protective interaction. The combination dramatically increases lung cancer risk.

questions.stem: Lung cancer can cause hoarseness through compression of which of the following nerves?

explanation.summary: Hoarseness in lung cancer is caused by compression of the vagus nerve (specifically the recurrent laryngeal nerve branch), which supplies the vocal cords. Phrenic nerve compression causes diaphragm paralysis and dyspnea.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hoarseness in lung cancer results from compression of the vagus nerve, specifically its recurrent laryngeal branch, which supplies the vocal cords. This occurs when the tumor extends into mediastinal structures. Separately, phrenic nerve compression causes diaphragm paralysis and dyspnea.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Phrenic nerve compression causes diaphragm paralysis and dyspnea, not hoarseness.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Brachial plexus compression causes arm pain (Pancoast tumor), not hoarseness.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sympathetic trunk compression causes Horner syndrome (miosis, ptosis, anhidrosis), not hoarseness.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Long thoracic nerve injury causes winged scapula, not hoarseness.

questions.stem: The most common sites of distant metastasis in lung cancer include all of the following EXCEPT which?

explanation.summary: The most common sites of distant lung cancer metastasis are lymph nodes, brain, bone, liver, contralateral lung, and adrenal gland. The spleen is not a common site of lung cancer metastasis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The most common sites of distant lung cancer metastasis are lymph nodes, brain, bone, liver, elsewhere in the lung, and the adrenal gland. The spleen is not among the common metastatic sites. Metastasis patterns vary by cancer typeâ€”SCLC favors liver (35%) and nervous system (47%), while NSCLC favors bone (39%) and respiratory system (22%).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Brain is a common metastatic site, especially for SCLC (47% nervous system metastases).
whyWrongByChoice.B: Bone is a common metastatic site, especially for NSCLC (39%). Bone pain can be an initial presenting symptom.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Liver is a common metastatic site, especially for SCLC (35%).
whyWrongByChoice.D: The adrenal gland is a common site of lung cancer metastasis.

questions.stem: Mesothelioma is sometimes confused with primary lung cancer. Which of the following best describes mesothelioma?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A malignant tumor of the pleural lining

choices.label: C
choices.text: A benign tumor of the bronchial epithelium

choices.label: D
choices.text: A neuroendocrine tumor of the lung

choices.label: E
choices.text: A secondary metastatic deposit from breast cancer

explanation.summary: Mesothelioma is a malignant tumor of serosal linings like the pleura and peritoneum. It is NOT a primary lung cancer but a cancer of the pleural lining surrounding the lung, strongly associated with asbestos exposure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mesothelioma is a malignant tumor of serosal linings like the pleura and peritoneum. It is not truly lung cancer but a cancer of the pleural lining that surrounds the lung. It is strongly associated with asbestos exposure. Asbestos particles migrate from the lung into the pleural sac, inducing malignant transformation of mesothelial cells.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mesothelioma arises from the pleura (mesothelial lining), not the lung parenchyma. Primary lung cancers (bronchogenic carcinomas) arise from the lung itself.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mesothelioma is malignant, not benign, and arises from the pleura, not the bronchial epithelium.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Neuroendocrine tumors of the lung include SCLC and carcinoid tumors. Mesothelioma is a distinct entity from the pleural lining.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mesothelioma is a primary tumor of the pleura, not a metastatic deposit from another cancer.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: A 68-year-old male with a long smoking history presents with persistent hoarseness and an enlarged cervical lymph node. Laryngoscopy reveals a mass on the larynx. Biopsy shows polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm and keratin pearls. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Most laryngeal cancers are squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), presenting with hoarseness and cervical lymphadenopathy in smokers. Histology shows polygonal cells with eosinophilic cytoplasm, keratin pearls, and pleomorphic nuclei.
explanation.whyCorrect: Most laryngeal cancers are squamous cell carcinoma. The presentation (persistent hoarseness, smoking history, cervical lymph node) and histology (polygonal cells, eosinophilic cytoplasm, keratin pearls, pleomorphic nuclei) are classic for laryngeal SCC. Smoking is the strongest risk factor. Laryngeal cancer accounts for one-third of head and neck cancers.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Olfactory neuroblastoma arises from olfactory epithelium and shows small round blue cells in a fibrovascular stroma, not keratin pearls.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma presents with nasal obstruction and cervical lymph nodes but histologically shows nests of epithelioid cells usually without keratinization, not the keratinizing SCC pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adenoid cystic carcinoma is a salivary gland tumor, not the typical laryngeal cancer. It shows a 'Swiss cheese' pattern histologically.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mucoepidermoid carcinoma is also a salivary gland tumor. Laryngeal cancer is predominantly SCC.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma is associated with which of the following infectious agents?

explanation.summary: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma is strongly associated with Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection, along with other risk factors including preserved/salted foods, tobacco, alcohol, and genetic predisposition.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma is strongly associated with EBV infection. Other risk factors include eating preserved foods (salted fish, foods with high nitrosamines), tobacco smoking, alcohol use, and genetic predisposition. The tumor shows EBV positivity on immunohistochemistry. NPC is more common in Southern China, Southeast Asia, North Africa, and the Arctic.

whyWrongByChoice.A: HPV is associated with laryngeal cancer and cervical cancer (oncoproteins E6/E7), not nasopharyngeal carcinoma. NPC is associated with EBV.
whyWrongByChoice.C: HSV is not associated with nasopharyngeal carcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CMV is not associated with nasopharyngeal carcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: HHV-8 is associated with Kaposi sarcoma, not nasopharyngeal carcinoma.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: A rare nasal tumor is biopsied and shows small round blue cells in a lobular architecture within a fibrovascular stroma, with Homer-Wright pseudorosettes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Olfactory neuroblastoma (esthesioneuroblastoma) shows small round blue cells in a lobular architecture with neuroblasts and neurofibrils in a fibrovascular stroma. Homer-Wright pseudorosettes and Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes are characteristic.
explanation.whyCorrect: Olfactory neuroblastoma is composed of small round blue cells with a lobular appearance, neuroblasts and neurofibril nests in a background of vascular fibrous stroma. Homer-Wright pseudorosettes and Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes are characteristic pathologic features of neuroblastomas. The tumor arises from olfactory epithelium.

whyWrongByChoice.A: NPC shows nests and sheets of epithelioid cells with prominent nucleoli, not small round blue cells with rosettes.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Laryngeal SCC shows polygonal cells with keratin pearls and pleomorphic nuclei, not small round blue cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sinonasal papilloma shows inverted epithelial growth with squamous, transitional, and columnar cells, not rosettes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: JNA shows fibroblastic cells in fibrous stroma with abundant capillaries, not small round blue cells.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: HPV-associated laryngeal cancer involves viral oncoproteins E6 and E7 that inactivate which of the following tumor suppressor proteins?

explanation.summary: HPV oncoproteins E6 and E7 inactivate tumor suppressor proteins p53 (E6) and pRb/retinoblastoma (E7), leading to uncontrolled cell division and cancer.
explanation.whyCorrect: HPV proteins E6 and E7 are oncoproteins that inactivate the patient's tumor suppressor proteins: E6 inactivates p53 (tumor protein 53) and E7 inactivates pRb (retinoblastoma protein). Blocking these tumor suppressors leads to uncontrolled progression of the cell cycle between G1 and S phase, uncontrolled cell division, and cancer.

whyWrongByChoice.A: BRCA1 and BRCA2 are tumor suppressors associated with breast and ovarian cancer, not the targets of HPV E6/E7.
whyWrongByChoice.C: APC is associated with familial adenomatous polyposis/colorectal cancer. DCC is associated with colon cancer. Neither is targeted by HPV E6/E7.
whyWrongByChoice.D: VHL is associated with von Hippel-Lindau disease and renal cell carcinoma. WT1 is associated with Wilms tumor. Neither is targeted by HPV.
whyWrongByChoice.E: NF1 and NF2 are associated with neurofibromatosis. They are not targeted by HPV oncoproteins.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma is treated differently from most head and neck cancers. Which of the following is the primary treatment modality?

explanation.summary: Unlike most head and neck cancers, NPC is primarily treated with radiotherapy and/or chemotherapy (cisplatin + gemcitabine) rather than surgery, which is very specialized and challenging for this tumor location.
explanation.whyCorrect: NPC is unique among these upper respiratory tumors because it is usually treated with radiotherapy and/or chemotherapy rather than surgery. Surgery in the nasopharynx is very specialized and challenging. The most common chemotherapy regimen is cisplatin + gemcitabine. The 5-year survival rate is 63-93% depending on stage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Surgical excision is not the primary treatment for NPC. Radiation therapy is first-line. This differs from olfactory neuroblastoma and laryngeal cancer where surgery is important.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chemotherapy alone is not standard. It is typically combined with radiation therapy for optimal outcomes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Watchful waiting is not appropriate for a malignant tumor like NPC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Laser ablation alone is not the standard treatment for NPC.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: Laryngeal cancers are classified by their location relative to the glottis. Which of the following locations accounts for approximately two-thirds of laryngeal cancers?

explanation.summary: Laryngeal cancers are classified as supraglottic (two-thirds of cases), glottic (one-third), or subglottic (1-2%). All commonly metastasize to local lymph nodes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Laryngeal cancers are classified by their location relative to the glottis: supraglottic (two-thirds of cases), glottic (one-third), or subglottic (1-2%). All types commonly metastasize to local lymph nodes. The location affects prognosis and treatment approach.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Glottic tumors account for approximately one-third of laryngeal cancers, not two-thirds.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Subglottic tumors are rare, accounting for only 1-2% of laryngeal cancers.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Transglottic is not one of the standard classifications mentioned in the brick.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Infraglottic is not a standard classification term used for laryngeal cancer location.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: The most common histologic type of nasopharyngeal carcinoma is which of the following?

explanation.summary: The non-keratinizing undifferentiated type accounts for 60-65% of NPC cases, making it the most common histologic subtype. It shows nests of epithelioid cells with prominent nucleoli infiltrated by benign lymphocytes.
explanation.whyCorrect: The non-keratinizing undifferentiated type is the most common histologic subtype of NPC, accounting for 60-65% of cases. Histologically, it shows nests and sheets of epithelioid-appearing cells that usually lack keratinization, with large cells showing prominent nucleoli and vesicular chromatin. Benign lymphocytes infiltrate the syncytial clusters of malignant epithelium.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The keratinizing type accounts for 20-25% of NPC, making it less common than the undifferentiated type.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma is rare (1-2% of NPC cases).
whyWrongByChoice.D: Adenocarcinoma is not a type of NPC. NPC arises from epithelial cells lining the nasopharynx.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Small cell carcinoma is a type of lung cancer, not NPC.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: Olfactory neuroblastoma can extend through the cribriform plate. Which of the following symptoms would this extension cause?

explanation.summary: Extension of olfactory neuroblastoma through the cribriform plate can cause anosmia (inability to smell) because the olfactory nerves pass through the cribriform plate. Other extensions can cause orbital symptoms and frontal headache.
explanation.whyCorrect: The cribriform plate is a bony structure through which olfactory nerve fibers pass from the nasal cavity to the olfactory bulb. Extension of olfactory neuroblastoma through the cribriform plate damages these olfactory nerves, causing anosmia (inability to smell). Other symptoms from local extension include orbital involvement (pain, proptosis, diplopia) and frontal headache.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hoarseness and dysphagia are symptoms of laryngeal cancer compressing vocal cords and esophagus, not cribriform plate extension.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hearing loss from ONB can occur through Eustachian tube obstruction (otitis media), not cribriform plate extension.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Facial paralysis would involve cranial nerve VII (facial nerve), which does not pass through the cribriform plate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tongue deviation would involve cranial nerve XII (hypoglossal), which is not affected by cribriform plate extension.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: The most common presenting complaint of nasopharyngeal carcinoma is which of the following?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neck mass from cervical lymph node metastasis

explanation.summary: While NPC may initially cause nasal obstruction and epistaxis, the most common presenting complaints are headache, diplopia, facial numbness (from cranial nerve involvement), and particularly a mass in the neck from cervical lymph node metastases.
explanation.whyCorrect: The clinical presentation of NPC depends on invasion depth and site. While there may initially be nasal obstruction and epistaxis, the most common presenting complaints are headache, diplopia, or facial numbness (from cranial nerve involvement) and a mass in the neck due to cervical lymph node metastases. Many patients present with the neck mass as their primary complaint.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Persistent hoarseness is the hallmark of laryngeal cancer, not NPC.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Unilateral nasal polyp is more associated with sinonasal papilloma, not NPC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While epistaxis can occur with NPC, the cervical lymph node mass is a more common presenting complaint.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anosmia is more associated with olfactory neuroblastoma extending through the cribriform plate, not NPC.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: For localized laryngeal cancer, the treatment approach aims to preserve which of the following functions?

explanation.summary: For tumors localized to the larynx, combined radiation and limited surgery (partial laryngectomy) are used to preserve vocal function. Total laryngectomy may be needed for advanced disease, resulting in voice loss.
explanation.whyCorrect: For tumors localized to the larynx, the treatment approach uses combined radiation and limited (organ-sparing) surgery, such as partial laryngectomy, to preserve vocal function. Voice loss is a challenging complication for patients requiring total laryngectomy, and voice retraining may be needed. Preservation of voice is a primary treatment goal when feasible.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Nasal airflow is affected by nasal tumors (ONB, NPC), not the primary concern in laryngeal cancer treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While swallowing can be affected, voice preservation is the primary function that organ-sparing laryngeal surgery aims to maintain.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Olfactory sensation is affected by olfactory neuroblastoma, not laryngeal cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Facial symmetry is not the primary concern in laryngeal cancer treatment.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: Which of the following is the most important risk factor for laryngeal cancer?

explanation.summary: Tobacco smoking is the most important risk factor for laryngeal cancer. Other risk factors include heavy alcohol use, HPV infection, and occupational exposures (asbestos, radiation, paint fumes). Incidence is dropping due to reduced smoking.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tobacco smoking is the most important risk factor for laryngeal cancer. Most patients with laryngeal cancer have a long history of cigarette smoking. The incidence in the US is dropping due to reduced smoking. Other risk factors include heavy alcohol use, HPV infection, and occupational exposures (asbestos, radiation, paint, gas fumes).

whyWrongByChoice.A: EBV is associated with nasopharyngeal carcinoma, not laryngeal cancer. Laryngeal cancer's viral association is with HPV.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Preserved food (salted fish, nitrosamines) is a risk factor for NPC, not laryngeal cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Asbestos exposure is a risk factor but is not the MOST important. Tobacco smoking is overwhelmingly the leading risk factor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chronic sinusitis is not a major risk factor for laryngeal cancer.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: Olfactory neuroblastoma arises from which of the following specialized tissues?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Olfactory epithelium of the nasal cavity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vocal fold mucosa of the larynx

explanation.summary: Olfactory neuroblastoma (esthesioneuroblastoma) is a malignant neuroendocrine tumor that arises from the olfactory epitheliumâ€”the specialized tissue in the nasal cavity associated with smell.
explanation.whyCorrect: Olfactory neuroblastoma (also called esthesioneuroblastoma) arises from the olfactory epithelium, the specialized neuroepithelial tissue in the nasal cavity associated with the sense of smell. It is a rare malignant neuroendocrine tumor (2% of all sinonasal tumors), highly differentiated, with a mean age at presentation of 53 years.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Squamous epithelium of the nasopharynx gives rise to nasopharyngeal carcinoma, not olfactory neuroblastoma.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Respiratory epithelium of the trachea gives rise to lower respiratory tract cancers, not ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vocal fold mucosa gives rise to laryngeal cancer (SCC), not ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Transitional epithelium of sinuses can give rise to sinonasal papilloma, not specifically ONB.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: Gemcitabine, used in NPC chemotherapy, acts by which of the following mechanisms?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inhibiting DNA synthesis as a pyrimidine analog

explanation.summary: Gemcitabine is an antimetabolite pyrimidine analog that inhibits DNA synthesis in the S phase of the cell cycle. Adverse effects include myelosuppression, fatigue, nausea, hair loss, and liver enzyme elevation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Gemcitabine is an antimetabolite chemotherapy agent, a pyrimidine analog (similar to cytarabine) that inhibits DNA synthesis in the S phase of the cell cycle. It is used with cisplatin for NPC. Adverse effects include myelosuppression, fatigue, nausea/vomiting, hair loss, liver enzyme elevation, and flu-like symptoms.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Topoisomerase II inhibition is the mechanism of etoposide and doxorubicin, not gemcitabine.
whyWrongByChoice.B: DNA crosslinking is the mechanism of cisplatin and other alkylating agents, not gemcitabine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Microtubule inhibition is the mechanism of vincristine (vinca alkaloids) and paclitaxel (taxanes), not gemcitabine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tyrosine kinase inhibition is the mechanism of targeted therapies like erlotinib and gefitinib, not gemcitabine.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma has a higher incidence in certain geographic regions. Which of the following areas has the highest incidence?

explanation.summary: NPC has a higher incidence in Southern China, Southeast Asia, North Africa and the Middle East, and the Arctic. These geographic patterns relate to EBV prevalence, dietary habits (preserved/salted foods), and genetic predisposition.
explanation.whyCorrect: The incidence of nasopharyngeal carcinoma is highest in Southern China, Southeast Asia, North Africa and the Middle East, and the Arctic. These geographic patterns are related to the combination of EBV prevalence, dietary habits (preserved foods high in nitrosamines), and genetic predisposition in these populations.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Northern Europe has relatively low NPC incidence compared to the high-risk areas.
whyWrongByChoice.B: North America (including the US) is a low-risk area for NPC. In the US, there is a bimodal age distribution when it does occur.
whyWrongByChoice.D: South America is not identified as a high-incidence region for NPC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Australia is not a high-incidence region for NPC.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: The 5-year survival rate for olfactory neuroblastoma ranges from 40% to 90%. Which of the following treatment approaches is standard for ONB?

explanation.summary: ONB is treated with surgery combined with adjuvant radiation therapy, chemotherapy (cisplatin + etoposide), or both. Targeted therapies and immunotherapies have not shown significant benefit in ONB.
explanation.whyCorrect: Olfactory neuroblastoma is treated with a combination of surgery and adjuvant radiation therapy, chemotherapy, or both. The most common chemotherapy regimen is cisplatin + etoposide. Targeted therapies and immunotherapies have not shown as much benefit in ONB, although clinical trials are ongoing. The wide survival range (40-90%) reflects the variability in stage at diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Radiation alone is not the standard approach. Surgery is a critical component of ONB treatment, combined with adjuvant therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cisplatin + gemcitabine is the NPC chemotherapy regimen. ONB uses cisplatin + etoposide, and chemotherapy is combined with surgery, not used alone.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Immunotherapy has not shown significant benefit in ONB and is not first-line.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Watchful waiting is not appropriate for an aggressive malignant tumor like ONB.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: Which of the following upper respiratory malignancies is the most common overall?

explanation.summary: Laryngeal cancer is the most common of the upper respiratory malignancies discussed, accounting for one-third of head and neck cancers, with an annual incidence of 3:100,000.
explanation.whyCorrect: Laryngeal cancer is the most common of the upper respiratory tract malignancies, accounting for a third of head and neck cancers with an annual incidence of 3:100,000. ONB is very rare (0.4 per million), and while NPC is significant in certain geographic areas, laryngeal cancer is more common overall.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ONB is very rare (2% of sinonasal tumors, 0.4 per million). It is not the most common upper respiratory malignancy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: NPC is significant in certain geographic regions but is not the most common upper respiratory malignancy overall.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sinonasal adenocarcinoma is rare and not the most common.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nasal NK/T-cell lymphoma is rare and not the most common upper respiratory malignancy.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: Cisplatin is used in chemotherapy regimens for all three upper respiratory malignancies discussed. Which of the following is cisplatin's primary mechanism of action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inhibition of DNA synthesis as a nucleotide analog

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cross-linking of DNA strands preventing replication

choices.label: E
choices.text: Inhibition of protein synthesis at the ribosome

explanation.summary: Cisplatin is a platinum-based chemotherapy agent that cross-links DNA strands, preventing DNA replication and transcription, ultimately leading to cell death. It is used in ONB (with etoposide), NPC (with gemcitabine), and laryngeal cancer.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cisplatin is a platinum-based alkylating agent that cross-links DNA strands (both intrastrand and interstrand), preventing DNA replication and transcription. This DNA damage triggers apoptosis. Cisplatin is a backbone of chemotherapy for upper respiratory malignancies: combined with etoposide for ONB, gemcitabine for NPC, and used alone or in combinations for laryngeal cancer.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Nucleotide analog activity describes gemcitabine, not cisplatin. Cisplatin directly cross-links DNA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Topoisomerase I inhibition is the mechanism of irinotecan and topotecan, not cisplatin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mitotic spindle blockade is the mechanism of vinca alkaloids (vincristine) and taxanes (paclitaxel), not cisplatin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ribosomal protein synthesis inhibition is the mechanism of antibiotics (macrolides, aminoglycosides), not cisplatin.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: A patient with laryngeal cancer may require total laryngectomy for advanced disease. Which of the following is the most challenging complication of this surgery?

explanation.summary: Voice loss is a challenging complication for patients who require total laryngectomy. Voice retraining may be required. This is less common with organ-sparing (partial laryngectomy) surgery.
explanation.whyCorrect: Voice loss is the most challenging complication of total laryngectomy. The larynx contains the vocal cords, so its complete removal eliminates the ability to produce voice normally. Voice retraining may be required. This complication drives the preference for organ-sparing surgery (partial laryngectomy) with radiation when feasible for localized tumors.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chronic sinusitis is not a typical complication of laryngectomy. It is more associated with nasal/sinus pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Epistaxis is associated with nasal tumors (ONB, NPC), not laryngectomy complications.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Anosmia is associated with olfactory nerve damage (ONB extending through cribriform plate), not laryngectomy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Facial nerve paralysis is not a typical complication of laryngectomy. The facial nerve (CN VII) is not in the surgical field.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: Olfactory neuroblastoma can rarely present with paraneoplastic syndromes. Which of the following has been reported?

explanation.summary: Rarely, patients with olfactory neuroblastoma can present with paraneoplastic syndromes including SIADH, hypercalcemia, and ectopic ACTH productionâ€”similar to other neuroendocrine tumors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Rarely, patients with olfactory neuroblastoma can present with paraneoplastic syndromes, including SIADH, hypercalcemia, and ectopic ACTH production. This is consistent with ONB's classification as a neuroendocrine tumor, which can produce ectopic hormones.

whyWrongByChoice.A: HUS is not a reported paraneoplastic syndrome of ONB. It is associated with E. coli O157:H7 infection.
whyWrongByChoice.C: TTP is not a typical paraneoplastic syndrome of ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia is not a characteristic paraneoplastic syndrome of ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nephrotic syndrome is not a characteristic paraneoplastic association with ONB.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the gross appearance of olfactory neuroblastoma?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Single pink or brown friable nasal mass

choices.label: E
choices.text: White keratotic plaque on vocal fold

explanation.summary: ONB usually presents grossly as a single pink or brown friable nasal mass within the nasal cavity. The most common symptom is nasal obstruction due to this mass.
explanation.whyCorrect: Olfactory neuroblastoma usually presents grossly as a single pink or brown friable nasal mass within the nasal cavity. Nasal obstruction due to the presence of this mass is the most common presenting symptom.

whyWrongByChoice.A: An ulcerated fungating laryngeal mass describes advanced laryngeal cancer, not ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A smooth violet lobulated mass describes juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA), not ONB.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bilateral translucent polyps describes common nasal polyps, which are benign and bilateral.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A white keratotic plaque on the vocal fold describes laryngeal leukoplakia or early SCC, not ONB.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: Laryngeal cancer has a higher prevalence in males and in which of the following racial groups in the United States?

explanation.summary: Laryngeal cancer has a higher prevalence in males and in Black patients. The incidence is dropping in the US due to reduced smoking rates.
explanation.whyCorrect: Laryngeal cancer has a higher prevalence in males and in Black patients. Historically, higher smoking rates in these demographic groups contributed to this disparity. The overall incidence in the United States is now dropping, likely due to reduced smoking.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While White patients develop laryngeal cancer, the prevalence is specifically noted to be higher in Black patients.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hispanic patients are not specifically identified as having higher laryngeal cancer prevalence.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Asian patients have lower rates of laryngeal cancer in the US. However, Asian populations (Southern China) have higher rates of nasopharyngeal carcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Native American patients are not specifically identified as having higher laryngeal cancer prevalence in this context.

questions.topicName: Malignant Tumors of the Upper Respiratory System
questions.stem: Laryngeal cancer is diagnosed using which of the following procedures as the primary diagnostic tool?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fine needle aspiration of the cervical node

explanation.summary: Laryngeal cancer is diagnosed by flexible laryngoscopy, during which the tumor is visualized and biopsied. CT/MRI are then used for staging to assess invasion and metastasis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Laryngeal cancer is usually diagnosed by flexible laryngoscopy, during which the tumor is directly visualized and biopsied for histologic confirmation. A thorough assessment of the neck and lymph nodes is also performed. After diagnosis, imaging (CT, MRI, PET) is used for staging to determine the extent of invasion and metastasis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CT scan is used for staging after diagnosis, not as the primary diagnostic tool. Laryngoscopy with biopsy is needed for tissue diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: PET scan is useful for staging, detection, and follow-up but is not the primary diagnostic procedure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: FNA of a cervical lymph node may confirm metastatic disease but does not directly diagnose the primary laryngeal tumor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There are no specific serum tumor markers used for diagnosing laryngeal cancer.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old smoker with a 40-pack-year history presents with confusion and weight loss. Serum sodium is 117 mEq/L. Chest X-ray shows a perihilar lung mass. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism causing his hyponatremia?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tumor secretion of ADH causing SIADH

explanation.summary: SCLC commonly secretes ectopic ADH, causing SIADH with increased water reabsorption and hyponatremia. SIADH occurs in ~10% of SCLC patients and is the most common paraneoplastic syndrome in this cancer.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC is derived from neuroendocrine progenitor cells that can secrete ectopic ADH (vasopressin). The resulting SIADH causes increased renal water reabsorption, leading to increased total body water and dilutional hyponatremia. This is the most common paraneoplastic syndrome in SCLC (10% of patients). Confusion from severe hyponatremia (117 mEq/L) is a common presenting symptom.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Renal salt wasting occurs in CNS pathology and Legionella infection, not typically as a paraneoplastic mechanism of SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While adrenal metastasis can occur in SCLC, the perihilar mass with severe hyponatremia in a heavy smoker points to SIADH from ectopic ADH production.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Polydipsia would not be the primary cause in the context of a perihilar mass in a heavy smoker. Ectopic ADH from SCLC is much more likely.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There is no mention of diuretic use. The perihilar mass and smoking history point to tumor-related SIADH.

questions.stem: SCLC is derived from neuroendocrine progenitor cells. Which of the following histologic features is characteristic of SCLC under the microscope?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Large cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm

choices.label: B
choices.text: Small cells with scant cytoplasm and salt-and-pepper chromatin

explanation.summary: SCLC shows small cells with scant cytoplasm, dispersed 'salt-and-pepper' chromatin (finely granular, speckled pattern), and inconspicuous nucleoliâ€”characteristic of neuroendocrine tumors.
explanation.whyCorrect: Under the microscope, SCLC is composed of small cells with scant cytoplasm. At higher power, the malignant cells show dispersed 'salt-and-pepper' chromatin (finely granular, speckled pattern with dark granules interspersed with lighter areas) and inconspicuous nucleoli. This chromatin pattern is characteristic of neuroendocrine tumors.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Large cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm would suggest large cell carcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma, not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Glandular acini formation is characteristic of adenocarcinoma, not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Keratin pearls with intercellular bridges are characteristic of squamous cell carcinoma, not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Clear cells with lipid-rich cytoplasm suggest renal cell carcinoma or certain adrenal tumors, not SCLC.

questions.stem: SCLC has the strongest association with tobacco smoking among all lung cancers. Approximately what percentage of SCLC occurs in nonsmokers?

explanation.summary: Only about 1% of SCLC occurs in nonsmokers, making it the lung cancer most strongly associated with smoking. This is compared to ~10% of all lung cancers occurring in nonsmokers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Among lung cancers, SCLC has the strongest association with tobacco smoking. Only about 1% of SCLC occurs in nonsmokers, compared to approximately 10% of all lung cancers. Smoking-induced somatic mutations in tumor suppressor genes (TP53, RB) and oncogene amplification (MYC) drive SCLC development.

whyWrongByChoice.B: 10% is the approximate rate of all lung cancers in nonsmokers, not SCLC specifically. SCLC's nonsmoker rate is even lower at ~1%.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 25% significantly overestimates nonsmoker SCLC cases.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 40% would mean nearly half of SCLC occurs without smoking, which is incorrect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 50% would mean equal rates in smokers and nonsmokers, which is far from reality for SCLC.

questions.stem: The Azzopardi effect is a histologic finding in SCLC. Which of the following best describes this phenomenon?

choices.label: B
choices.text: DNA encrustation of blood vessel walls from tumor necrosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Keratin pearl formation in necrotic areas

choices.label: E
choices.text: Palisading of nuclei at the tumor periphery

explanation.summary: The Azzopardi effect occurs when rapidly growing SCLC outgrows its blood supply, causing central necrosis. Released cellular debris including DNA adheres to and 'incrusts' nearby blood vessel walls, visible as basophilic deposits.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC tumors are high grade with rapid growth that often outstrips their blood supply, causing central necrosis. Released cellular debris, including DNA, adheres to and 'incrusts' the walls of nearby blood vessels, visible histologically as basophilic granular deposits. This is the Azzopardi effect, seen in SCLC and other high-grade neoplasms.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Glandular differentiation is a feature of adenocarcinoma, not SCLC. SCLC shows neuroendocrine differentiation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Keratin pearl formation is characteristic of squamous cell carcinoma, not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Rosette formation (Homer-Wright pseudorosettes) is seen in olfactory neuroblastoma and other neuroblastic tumors, not specifically the Azzopardi effect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Nuclear palisading is seen in schwannomas and some other tumors, not the Azzopardi effect of SCLC.

questions.stem: The most common chemotherapy regimen for SCLC is cisplatin combined with which of the following agents?

explanation.summary: The most common chemotherapy regimen for SCLC is cisplatin + etoposide. Etoposide is a topoisomerase II inhibitor that prevents religation of DNA double-strand breaks during the S/early G2 phase.
explanation.whyCorrect: The standard chemotherapy regimen for SCLC is cisplatin + etoposide. Cisplatin cross-links DNA, and etoposide is a topoisomerase II inhibitor that inhibits religation of double-strand breaks during the S or early G2 phase of the cell cycle. Adverse effects of etoposide include myelosuppression, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, and diarrhea.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cisplatin + gemcitabine is the regimen for nasopharyngeal carcinoma, not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Paclitaxel may be used in some lung cancer regimens but cisplatin + etoposide is the standard for SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Doxorubicin is used in other cancers (breast, lymphoma) but is not the standard SCLC combination with cisplatin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Methotrexate is an antimetabolite used in other cancers but is not part of standard SCLC chemotherapy.

questions.stem: SCLC tumor cells express neuroendocrine markers on immunohistochemistry. Which of the following markers confirm neuroendocrine differentiation?

explanation.summary: SCLC tumor cells express neuroendocrine markers including chromogranin and synaptophysin, confirming their origin from neuroendocrine progenitor cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because SCLC is derived from neuroendocrine progenitor cells, tumor cells often express neuroendocrine markers such as chromogranin (a secretory granule protein) and synaptophysin (a synaptic vesicle protein). These immunohistochemical markers help confirm the neuroendocrine differentiation and distinguish SCLC from other lung cancer types.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cytokeratin 5/6 and p63 are markers of squamous cell differentiation, not neuroendocrine.
whyWrongByChoice.C: TTF-1 and napsin A are markers of lung adenocarcinoma, not specifically neuroendocrine differentiation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CD20 and CD79a are B cell markers used in lymphoma diagnosis, not lung cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: S-100 and HMB-45 are melanoma markers, not neuroendocrine markers.

questions.stem: Approximately 70% of SCLC patients present with overt metastatic disease. Which of the following is the most common site of SCLC metastasis?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lymph nodes, brain, liver, bone, and adrenals

explanation.summary: SCLC frequently metastasizes to lymph nodes, brain/spinal cord, liver, bone, bone marrow, adrenal glands, and skin. ~70% of patients present with metastatic disease at diagnosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC has a particular propensity to spread widely. The most common metastatic sites include lymph nodes, brain/spinal cord, liver, bone, bone marrow, adrenal glands, and skin. Approximately 70% of patients present with overt metastatic disease at diagnosis, explaining why surgery is rarely beneficial.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Skin can be a metastatic site for SCLC, but metastasis occurs at multiple sites simultaneously, not skin only.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Kidneys are not the most common exclusive metastatic site. SCLC spreads to multiple organs.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Spleen and thymus are not the primary metastatic sites for SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: GI tract alone is not the predominant metastatic pattern. SCLC metastasizes to liver, brain, bone, and adrenals preferentially.

questions.stem: SCLC most commonly appears on imaging as which of the following?

explanation.summary: SCLC most commonly shows a central (perihilar) mass on imaging, similar to squamous cell carcinoma. Because of its central location, bronchoscopy is the preferred biopsy method.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC most commonly presents as a central (perihilar) mass on imaging. This central location is important for diagnosis because it makes bronchoscopy the preferred method for biopsy, as transthoracic needle biopsy risks damaging adjacent mediastinal structures.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Peripheral coin lesions are more characteristic of adenocarcinoma. SCLC is centrally located.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bilateral ground-glass opacities are more characteristic of viral pneumonia or PCP, not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cavitary upper lobe lesions are more associated with reactivation TB, squamous cell carcinoma, or Nocardia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Calcified mediastinal lymph nodes suggest prior granulomatous disease (TB, histoplasmosis), not SCLC.

questions.stem: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome occurs in approximately 3% of SCLC patients. The muscle weakness in this syndrome is caused by autoantibodies against which of the following targets?

explanation.summary: Lambert-Eaton syndrome is caused by autoantibodies against presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels at the neuromuscular junction, reducing acetylcholine release and causing proximal muscle weakness.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome is caused by autoantibodies produced by the tumor that target presynaptic calcium channels on neurons at the neuromuscular junction. This reduces calcium-dependent acetylcholine release, leading to proximal muscle weakness (particularly lower extremities), fatigue, diaphragmatic weakness, and bulbar symptoms. It occurs in about 3% of SCLC patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Autoantibodies against postsynaptic acetylcholine receptors cause myasthenia gravis (MG), not Lambert-Eaton. MG is associated with thymoma, not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (drugs like pyridostigmine) treat MG. Autoantibodies against the enzyme are not the mechanism of Lambert-Eaton.
whyWrongByChoice.D: MuSK antibodies cause a variant of myasthenia gravis, not Lambert-Eaton syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Voltage-gated sodium channel antibodies are not the target in Lambert-Eaton. Calcium channels are the target.

questions.stem: SCLC has mutations in tumor suppressor genes TP53 and RB. Additionally, which of the following oncogenes is often amplified in SCLC?

explanation.summary: The MYC gene family (C-MYC, N-MYC, L-MYC) is often amplified in SCLC, driving tumor cell proliferation. Some tumors also overexpress BCL2, suppressing apoptosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In SCLC, tumor suppressor genes TP53 and RB are commonly nonfunctional/dysfunctional. Additionally, the MYC gene is often amplified, driving tumor cell proliferation. Some SCLC tumors also overexpress BCL2 (an anti-apoptotic protein), further suppressing programmed cell death and allowing cancerous cells to survive.

whyWrongByChoice.A: EGFR mutations are associated with adenocarcinoma (NSCLC), not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ALK rearrangements are associated with adenocarcinoma (NSCLC), not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: BRAF mutations are associated with adenocarcinoma (NSCLC) and melanoma, not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: HER2 amplification is associated with breast cancer and some gastric cancers, not typically SCLC.

questions.stem: Most patients with SCLC are not surgical candidates. Which of the following best explains why surgery is rarely an option?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The tumor is too small to locate surgically

choices.label: B
choices.text: Most patients present with metastatic disease at diagnosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: SCLC only occurs in patients too elderly for surgery

choices.label: E
choices.text: The tumor is always located in the pleural space

explanation.summary: Approximately 70% of SCLC patients present with metastatic disease at diagnosis. With widespread disease, surgical resection of the primary tumor would not be curative, so chemotherapy and radiation are the mainstays of treatment.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC patients often have metastatic disease at the time of diagnosis (~70%), making surgical resection unlikely to be beneficial. Only patients with a single lung mass and no metastatic spread can undergo surgery with postoperative chemotherapy, but this is uncommon. Chemotherapy with or without radiation is the main treatment for most SCLC patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SCLC typically presents as a visible central mass, not a tumor too small to find.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no issue with SCLC and anesthesia. The problem is metastatic disease making surgery non-curative.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While SCLC typically presents in older adults (smokers), age alone is not the primary reason surgery is avoided. Metastatic disease is the key factor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: SCLC is centrally located (perihilar), not in the pleural space.

questions.stem: The median survival for SCLC with limited disease (cancer in one lung or nearby lymph nodes) is approximately which of the following?

explanation.summary: Median survival for limited SCLC is 15-20 months with treatment. For extensive disease (metastatic), median survival is 8-13 months. SCLC has the poorest prognosis among lung cancers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Due to SCLC's aggressive nature, median survival is 15-20 months for limited disease (cancer in one lung or nearby lymph nodes) and 8-13 months for extensive disease (cancer in the other lung or metastasis to distant organs). Despite initial response to chemotherapy, recurrence is common and prognosis remains poor.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 3-5 months would be even worse than the already poor prognosis. Limited SCLC median survival is 15-20 months.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 8-13 months is the median survival for extensive (widely metastatic) SCLC, not limited disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 3-5 years is too optimistic for SCLC. Even limited disease has median survival of only 15-20 months.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 10+ years greatly overestimates SCLC survival.

questions.stem: Bronchial carcinoid tumors differ from SCLC in that carcinoids are which of the following?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Exclusively found in the peripheral lung

explanation.summary: Bronchial carcinoids are low-grade, well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors, in contrast to SCLC which is high-grade and poorly differentiated. Both express neuroendocrine markers and have neuroendocrine morphology.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bronchial carcinoid tumors are low-grade/well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors, in contrast to SCLC which is high-grade/poorly differentiated. Carcinoids are typically located in central airways and cause airway obstruction symptoms. Like SCLC, they express neuroendocrine markers and show neuroendocrine morphology, but their clinical behavior is far less aggressive.

whyWrongByChoice.A: High-grade and poorly differentiated describes SCLC, not carcinoid tumors. Carcinoids are low-grade.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Both carcinoids and SCLC are neuroendocrine tumors, not squamous-derived.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carcinoids are typically central (in central airways), not peripheral.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carcinoids are not specifically associated with asbestos. SCLC is associated with smoking.

questions.stem: Etoposide, part of the standard SCLC regimen, exerts its anticancer effect by inhibiting which of the following enzymes?

explanation.summary: Etoposide is a topoisomerase II inhibitor. It prevents religation of double-strand DNA breaks during the S/early G2 phase, leading to DNA damage and cell death.
explanation.whyCorrect: Etoposide is a topoisomerase II inhibitor that prevents the religation of double-strand DNA breaks created by the enzyme during the S phase or early G2 phase of the cell cycle. This leads to accumulation of DNA damage and ultimately cell death. Adverse effects include myelosuppression, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, and diarrhea.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Topoisomerase I inhibition is the mechanism of irinotecan and topotecan, not etoposide.
whyWrongByChoice.C: DNA gyrase is the target of fluoroquinolones in bacteria. Etoposide targets human topoisomerase II in cancer cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: RNA polymerase inhibition is the mechanism of rifampin (antibacterial), not etoposide.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thymidylate synthase inhibition is the mechanism of 5-fluorouracil, not etoposide.

questions.stem: A patient with SCLC and severe, symptomatic hyponatremia (Na 110 mEq/L) presents with seizures. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate treatment for the hyponatremia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chemotherapy to treat the underlying tumor

explanation.summary: Severe, symptomatic hyponatremia with seizures requires emergency treatment with hypertonic saline. While chemotherapy will address the underlying SIADH over weeks, acute symptomatic hyponatremia is life-threatening.
explanation.whyCorrect: Severe, symptomatic hyponatremia (seizures, confusion) requires immediate treatment with hypertonic saline to raise serum sodium and prevent neurological damage. While chemotherapy will ultimately address the underlying SIADH by treating the tumor (hyponatremia usually resolves within several weeks of starting chemo), acute treatment of life-threatening hyponatremia takes priority.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chemotherapy addresses the underlying cause (tumor-secreted ADH) but takes weeks to reduce sodium. Acute seizures from hyponatremia require immediate correction with hypertonic saline.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fluid restriction is used for mild, chronic SIADH but is insufficient for acute symptomatic hyponatremia with seizures.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ADH receptor agonists would worsen SIADH by enhancing ADH effects. ADH receptor antagonists (vaptans) could be considered but hypertonic saline is standard for acute emergencies.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Isotonic normal saline alone may not adequately correct severe hyponatremia quickly enough in the setting of seizures.

questions.stem: Cushing syndrome as a paraneoplastic manifestation of SCLC is caused by ectopic secretion of which of the following hormones?

explanation.summary: Ectopic ACTH secretion by SCLC (1-5% of patients) stimulates the adrenal glands to produce excess cortisol, causing Cushing syndrome with weight gain, muscle weakness, thin skin, and truncal obesity.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC can secrete ectopic ACTH (1-5% of patients), which stimulates the adrenal glands to produce excess cortisol. This causes Cushing syndrome with weight gain, muscle weakness, thin and fragile skin, truncal obesity, and other cushingoid features. This is less common than SIADH (10%) but is an important paraneoplastic syndrome to recognize.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ADH secretion causes SIADH and hyponatremia, not Cushing syndrome. ADH is the most common paraneoplastic hormone in SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.B: PTHrP causes hypercalcemia and is most associated with squamous cell carcinoma, not Cushing syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Growth hormone excess causes acromegaly, not Cushing syndrome. Ectopic GH is not a common SCLC paraneoplastic.
whyWrongByChoice.E: TSH secretion by tumors is extremely rare and is not a recognized SCLC paraneoplastic syndrome.

questions.stem: SCLC is best biopsied by bronchoscopy rather than transthoracic needle biopsy. Which of the following best explains this preference?

choices.label: A
choices.text: SCLC cells are too small for needle biopsy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Central tumor location makes needle biopsy riskier

choices.label: E
choices.text: SCLC only grows within the bronchial lumen

explanation.summary: SCLC is centrally oriented in the thorax (perihilar mass). Transthoracic needle biopsy risks damaging adjacent mediastinal structures. Bronchoscopy allows safer access to the centrally located tumor.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because most SCLC is centrally oriented in the thorax (perihilar location), bronchoscopy provides safer access to the tumor for biopsy. Transthoracic needle biopsy carries risk of damaging adjacent mediastinal structures when targeting a central mass.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cell size is not the issue. Needle biopsies can sample small cells. The concern is the central location of the tumor.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tissue sample size is not the primary reason. Safety considerations due to the tumor's central location drive the preference.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While tumor seeding is a theoretical concern, the primary reason for avoiding transthoracic needle biopsy is the risk of mediastinal injury with centrally located tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: SCLC does not only grow within the bronchial lumen. It forms a mass that invades surrounding tissue, but is centrally located.

questions.stem: SCLC overexpression of BCL2 contributes to tumor survival by which of the following mechanisms?

explanation.summary: BCL2 is an anti-apoptotic protein. Its overexpression in SCLC suppresses programmed cell death (apoptosis), allowing cancerous cells to survive despite accumulating DNA damage.
