explanation.whyCorrect: Some SCLC tumors overexpress BCL2, an anti-apoptotic protein. BCL2 suppresses programmed cell death (apoptosis), allowing cancerous cells to survive despite DNA damage. Combined with loss of tumor suppressors (TP53, RB) and MYC amplification, BCL2 overexpression contributes to the aggressive behavior of SCLC.

whyWrongByChoice.A: BCL2 does not promote DNA repair. It prevents apoptosis, allowing cells with damaged DNA to survive.
whyWrongByChoice.C: BCL2 does not directly increase angiogenesis. Other factors (VEGF) drive new blood vessel formation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: BCL2's mechanism is anti-apoptotic, not immune evasion. Immune checkpoint molecules (PD-L1) are involved in immune evasion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: BCL2 does not activate cell cycle checkpoints. TP53 and RB are the checkpoint regulators. BCL2 prevents the death of cells that bypass these checkpoints.

questions.stem: Immunotherapy agents atezolizumab and pembrolizumab may be added to chemotherapy for SCLC. These agents target which of the following immune checkpoint pathways?

explanation.summary: Atezolizumab (anti-PD-L1) and pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1) block the PD-1/PD-L1 immune checkpoint pathway, allowing T cells to attack tumor cells that express PD-L1 to evade immune detection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Atezolizumab and pembrolizumab are immune checkpoint inhibitors that target the PD-1/PD-L1 pathway. Atezolizumab blocks PD-L1 on tumor cells, while pembrolizumab blocks PD-1 on T cells. By blocking this checkpoint, T cells are reactivated to attack tumor cells. These agents may be added to cisplatin/etoposide chemotherapy for SCLC based on genetic testing results.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CTLA-4 pathway is targeted by ipilimumab, not atezolizumab or pembrolizumab.
whyWrongByChoice.C: CD28/B7 costimulation is a T cell activation pathway, not the target of these checkpoint inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: IL-2 receptor pathway is targeted by different immunotherapies (basiliximab, aldesleukin), not PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: TNF-Î± signaling is targeted by anti-TNF biologics (infliximab, adalimumab) for autoimmune diseases, not cancer immunotherapy.

questions.stem: All SCLC tumors are considered which of the following grades?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Benign with potential for malignant transformation

explanation.summary: All SCLC tumors are high grade with a high mitotic rate and rapid growth. They often outgrow their blood supply, leading to central necrosis (Azzopardi effect). This aggressive behavior explains the poor prognosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: All small cell lung tumors are high grade, with a high mitotic rate and rapid growth. They frequently outgrow their blood supply, causing central necrosis. This aggressive biology explains why SCLC is the most aggressive lung cancer type, often metastatic at diagnosis, with median survival of only 8-20 months.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SCLC is never low grade. Bronchial carcinoids are the low-grade neuroendocrine tumors. SCLC is always high grade.
whyWrongByChoice.B: SCLC is not intermediate grade. It is uniformly high grade.
whyWrongByChoice.D: All SCLC is high grade regardless of stage. Grade and stage are different concepts.
whyWrongByChoice.E: SCLC is always malignant. Benign neuroendocrine tumors of the lung are carcinoids, not SCLC.

questions.stem: SCLC is thought to originate from which of the following cell types in the bronchial wall?

explanation.summary: SCLC originates from bronchial neuroendocrine cells, which are normal constituents of the airway that secrete various amines and polypeptide hormones. This origin explains the neuroendocrine markers and paraneoplastic hormone secretion.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCLC is thought to originate from bronchial neuroendocrine cells, which are normal constituents of the airway that secrete various amines and polypeptide hormones like DOPA. This neuroendocrine origin explains why SCLC cells express neuroendocrine markers (chromogranin, synaptophysin) and can secrete ectopic hormones (ADH, ACTH) causing paraneoplastic syndromes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ciliated columnar epithelial cells are the predominant cell type in the respiratory epithelium but do not give rise to SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Goblet cells produce mucus and do not give rise to SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Type II pneumocytes produce surfactant and are the cell of origin for some adenocarcinomas, not SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Basal stem cells can give rise to squamous cell carcinoma (through squamous metaplasia), not specifically SCLC.

questions.stem: When the cancer is limited (confined to one lung and nearby lymph nodes), SCLC is treated with chemotherapy combined with radiation, with the goal of which of the following?

explanation.summary: For limited SCLC, chemotherapy and radiation together are used as initial therapy with the goal of curing the cancer. For extensive disease, chemotherapy alone is used to slow growth, reduce symptoms, and improve survival (not curative).
explanation.whyCorrect: For limited SCLC (cancer in only one lung or nearby lymph nodes), chemotherapy and radiation therapy can be used together as initial therapy with the goal of curing the cancer. However, for extensive (widespread) disease, chemotherapy is used alone to slow cancer growth, reduce symptoms, and improve survival, without the expectation of cure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: In limited disease, the treatment goal is curative, not purely palliative. Palliation is the goal for extensive disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The combined chemo-radiation approach is used to cure the cancer, not to prevent surgical complications.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While paraneoplastic symptoms do improve with treatment, the primary goal for limited disease is cancer cure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Most SCLC patients do not undergo surgery. The chemo-radiation regimen is the definitive treatment for limited disease, not preparation for surgery.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old woman with a nagging cough and hemoptysis has an elevated serum calcium. Chest X-ray reveals a central pulmonary nodule. Biopsy shows sheets of malignant squamous epithelium with keratin pearls and intercellular bridging. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Squamous cell carcinoma shows sheets of malignant squamous epithelium with keratin pearls and intercellular bridging. It is centrally located and associated with hypercalcemia (PTHrP). Strong smoking association.
explanation.whyCorrect: The central location, keratin pearls, intercellular bridging, and hypercalcemia are classic for squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the lung. SCC originates from flat squamous epithelial cells lining the airways, typically grows centrally, and is associated with PTHrP-mediated hypercalcemia as a paraneoplastic syndrome. SCC has the strongest smoking association among NSCLC subtypes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenocarcinoma shows glandular differentiation (mucin-producing glands/ducts), not keratin pearls. It is typically peripheral, not central.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Large cell carcinoma shows large cells with prominent nucleoli but lacks squamous or glandular differentiation by definition.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Small cell carcinoma shows small cells with scant cytoplasm and salt-and-pepper chromatin, not keratin pearls.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carcinoid tumors are low-grade neuroendocrine tumors with neuroendocrine markers, not keratin pearls.

questions.stem: Adenocarcinoma is the most common subtype of NSCLC and is also the most common lung cancer in which of the following patient populations?

explanation.summary: While smoking is a risk factor for all lung cancers, adenocarcinoma is the most common lung cancer subtype detected in nonsmokers. It arises from mucus-producing epithelial cells and can occur without smoking exposure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pulmonary adenocarcinoma is the most common lung cancer subtype detected in nonsmokers. While smoking is still a strong risk factor, adenocarcinoma can develop without smoking exposure. This is in contrast to squamous cell carcinoma and SCLC, which have even stronger smoking associations.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Heavy smokers are at risk for all lung cancers but squamous cell carcinoma and SCLC have the strongest smoking associations. The specific distinction is that adenocarcinoma is most common in nonsmokers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Asbestos exposure increases risk for lung cancer (especially with concurrent smoking) and mesothelioma, but nonsmoker status is the specific population where adenocarcinoma predominates.
whyWrongByChoice.D: COPD is a risk factor for lung cancer broadly, not specifically adenocarcinoma in nonsmokers.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Age is a risk factor for all lung cancers, not specifically adenocarcinoma in nonsmokers.

questions.stem: Adenocarcinoma and large cell carcinoma are typically found in which location within the lung?

explanation.summary: Adenocarcinoma and large cell carcinoma are typically peripheral lung tumors. In contrast, squamous cell carcinoma and SCLC are centrally located (hilar). Location provides a clue to histologic subtype.
explanation.whyCorrect: Adenocarcinomas and large cell carcinomas are most typically found in the lung periphery. In contrast, squamous cell carcinomas are usually found more centrally in the bronchial airways (similar to SCLC). This location difference provides an important clue to the underlying histology when evaluating imaging.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Central (hilar) location is characteristic of squamous cell carcinoma and SCLC, not adenocarcinoma or large cell carcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The pleural surface is the location of mesothelioma, not typically adenocarcinoma or large cell carcinoma primary tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mediastinal lymph nodes may be involved by metastasis but are not the typical primary tumor location.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The tracheal bifurcation (carina) is not the typical primary site of adenocarcinoma or large cell carcinoma.

questions.stem: A patient with lung adenocarcinoma has molecular testing revealing an EGFR mutation. Which of the following targeted therapies would be most appropriate?

explanation.summary: For EGFR-mutant adenocarcinoma, EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors like osimertinib terminate cancer cell growth by specifically targeting the mutated receptor. These work better than conventional chemotherapy for EGFR-positive tumors.
explanation.whyCorrect: For lung adenocarcinoma with EGFR mutations, specific tyrosine kinase inhibitors targeting EGFR (osimertinib) are indicated. These drugs terminate cancer cell growth by specifically targeting the mutated EGFR receptor. They work better than conventional chemotherapy for EGFR-positive tumors. Adverse effects include skin issues, mouth sores, fatigue, and muscle/joint pain.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alectinib targets ALK, not EGFR. It would be appropriate for ALK-rearranged adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pembrolizumab is a PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitor. While it may be used in NSCLC, EGFR-mutant tumors are specifically treated with EGFR TKIs first.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cisplatin is a general chemotherapy agent. While it may be part of the regimen, the targeted EGFR TKI is the specific therapy for EGFR-mutant tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Etoposide is used in SCLC (with cisplatin), not as targeted therapy for EGFR-mutant adenocarcinoma.

questions.stem: The progression from normal bronchial epithelium to invasive squamous cell carcinoma follows a stepwise sequence. Which of the following correctly orders these stages?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dysplasia â†’ metaplasia â†’ carcinoma in situ â†’ invasion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Metaplasia â†’ dysplasia â†’ carcinoma in situ â†’ invasion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hyperplasia â†’ metaplasia â†’ invasion â†’ carcinoma in situ

choices.label: D
choices.text: Carcinoma in situ â†’ metaplasia â†’ dysplasia â†’ invasion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Invasion â†’ metaplasia â†’ dysplasia â†’ carcinoma in situ

explanation.summary: The progression to invasive SCC follows: normal epithelium â†’ squamous metaplasia (from smoking) â†’ squamous dysplasia â†’ carcinoma in situ (full-thickness dysplasia) â†’ invasive SCC (basement membrane penetration).
explanation.whyCorrect: Squamous cell carcinoma develops through a stepwise progression: normal bronchial columnar epithelium â†’ squamous metaplasia (from cigarette smoke exposure) â†’ squamous dysplasia (with continued exposure) â†’ carcinoma in situ (full-thickness dysplasia without basement membrane invasion) â†’ invasive squamous cell carcinoma (basement membrane penetration with ability to invade and metastasize).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dysplasia does not precede metaplasia. Metaplasia (change in cell type) occurs first, followed by dysplasia (abnormal cell growth) within the metaplastic epithelium.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Invasion does not precede carcinoma in situ. CIS is defined as full-thickness dysplasia WITHOUT invasion; invasion occurs as the next step.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CIS does not precede metaplasia. The sequence starts with metaplasia and progresses toward CIS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Invasion is the final step, not the first.

questions.stem: Lung adenocarcinoma can be distinguished from metastatic adenocarcinoma to the lung by immunohistochemical staining. Which of the following markers are positive in primary lung adenocarcinoma?

explanation.summary: Primary lung adenocarcinoma stains positive for TTF-1 (thyroid transcription factor-1), CK7 (cytokeratin 7), and Napsin A. These markers distinguish it from adenocarcinoma metastatic to the lung from other organs.
explanation.whyCorrect: Primary lung adenocarcinoma is distinguished from metastatic adenocarcinoma by immunohistochemistry. Lung adenocarcinoma stains positive for TTF-1 (thyroid transcription factor-1), CK7 (cytokeratin 7), and Napsin A. Metastatic adenocarcinoma from other organs (stomach, colon) would be negative for these lung-specific markers.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CK5/6 and p63 are markers of squamous cell differentiation (SCC stains positive for CK7, CK5/6, p40, p63), not adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chromogranin and synaptophysin are neuroendocrine markers for SCLC and carcinoid tumors, not adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CD20 and CD79a are B cell markers for lymphoma, not lung cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: S-100 and HMB-45 are melanoma markers.

questions.stem: KRAS mutations are found in approximately 25% of lung adenocarcinomas. These mutations affect a GTPase-coding gene and are associated with which of the following prognoses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excellent prognosis with high cure rates

explanation.summary: KRAS mutations (found in ~25% of adenocarcinomas) affect a GTPase-coding gene and are associated with poor survival. Mutations in KRAS contribute to malignant transformation and reduce overall survival by about 25%.
explanation.whyCorrect: KRAS mutations are found in approximately 25% of lung adenocarcinoma cases and affect a GTPase-coding gene involved in cell signaling. KRAS mutations are associated with poor survivalâ€”they contribute to malignant transformation of cells and reduce overall survival by about 25%.

whyWrongByChoice.A: KRAS mutations are associated with poor survival, not excellent prognosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: KRAS mutations do affect prognosisâ€”they are associated with worse outcomes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While newer KRAS-targeted therapies are being developed, KRAS mutations have historically been associated with poor prognosis, not improved immunotherapy response.
whyWrongByChoice.E: KRAS-mutant tumors do not spontaneously regress. They tend to be aggressive.

questions.stem: Large cell carcinoma can be considered a diagnosis of exclusion. Which of the following histologic features define it?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Large cells with prominent nucleoli lacking squamous or glandular differentiation

explanation.summary: Large cell carcinoma is an undifferentiated malignant epithelial tumor with large cells, large nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and moderate cytoplasmâ€”but lacking evidence of glandular or squamous differentiation or SCLC features.
explanation.whyCorrect: Large cell carcinoma is defined by what it is NOT: it lacks glandular differentiation (adenocarcinoma), squamous differentiation (SCC), and features of small cell carcinoma. It shows large cells with large nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and moderate amounts of cytoplasm. It is a diagnosis of exclusion when other NSCLC subtypes are ruled out histologically.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Glandular formation with mucin production defines adenocarcinoma, not large cell carcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Keratin pearls with intercellular bridging define squamous cell carcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Small cells with salt-and-pepper chromatin define SCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Well-differentiated neuroendocrine morphology describes carcinoid tumors, not large cell carcinoma (though neuroendocrine variants of LCC exist).

questions.stem: The overall 5-year survival rate for NSCLC is approximately which of the following?

explanation.summary: The overall 5-year survival for NSCLC is about 25%. However, if detected early and localized to the lung, survival increases to ~50%. Distant metastasis drops survival to ~6%.
explanation.whyCorrect: Despite newer therapies, NSCLC generally has a poor prognosis. The overall 5-year survival rate is about 25%, but increases to approximately 50% if the cancer is detected early and is localized to the lung. Distant metastasis is associated with a 5-year survival of only about 6%. These numbers are improving with modern targeted therapies.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 5% is the 5-year survival for distant metastatic disease (~6%), not overall NSCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: 50% is the survival rate for early, localized NSCLC, not overall.
whyWrongByChoice.D: 75% overestimates the overall 5-year survival for NSCLC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 90% greatly overestimates NSCLC survival.

questions.stem: Alectinib is a targeted therapy used for which specific molecular alteration in lung adenocarcinoma?

explanation.summary: Alectinib is an ALK tyrosine kinase inhibitor used for lung adenocarcinoma with ALK rearrangement. ALK-positive patients are often younger, light or nonsmokers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alectinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor specifically targeting ALK (anaplastic lymphoma kinase). It is used for lung adenocarcinoma patients with ALK rearrangement, who tend to be younger, light or nonsmokers. Common adverse effects include fatigue, swelling, constipation, muscle pain, weight gain, vision changes, and photosensitivity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: EGFR mutations are treated with osimertinib (EGFR TKI), not alectinib.
whyWrongByChoice.B: KRAS mutations have been historically difficult to target, though newer KRAS inhibitors are being developed. Alectinib targets ALK.
whyWrongByChoice.D: TP53 deletion does not have a specific targeted therapy. It affects tumor suppressor function broadly.
whyWrongByChoice.E: BRAF V600E mutations are treated with BRAF inhibitors (dabrafenib/trametinib), not alectinib.

questions.stem: SCC of the lung originates from which of the following cell types?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Flat squamous epithelial cells of the airways

explanation.summary: Squamous cell carcinoma originates in the flat squamous epithelial cells lining the lungs' airways. It develops through squamous metaplasia â†’ dysplasia â†’ CIS â†’ invasive carcinoma from chronic cigarette smoke exposure.
explanation.whyCorrect: SCC originates in the flat squamous epithelial cells lining the lungs' airways. Normally, the bronchial epithelium is columnar, but chronic cigarette smoke exposure causes squamous metaplasia, which can progress through dysplasia to carcinoma.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mucus-producing epithelial cells give rise to adenocarcinoma, not SCC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neuroendocrine progenitor cells give rise to SCLC and carcinoid tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Type II pneumocytes produce surfactant and are involved in some adenocarcinomas, not SCC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Alveolar macrophages are immune cells that do not give rise to lung carcinomas.

questions.stem: Squamous cell carcinoma in situ differs from invasive SCC by which of the following criteria?

explanation.summary: SCC in situ has full-thickness epithelial dysplasia with the same cytologic features as invasive carcinoma but has NOT penetrated the basement membrane. Once it invades beyond the basement membrane, it becomes invasive SCC.
explanation.whyCorrect: Squamous cell carcinoma in situ is defined as full-thickness dysplasia of the epithelium with the same cytologic nuclear features as invasive carcinoma, BUT the cells have NOT gained the ability to invade beyond the basement membrane. Once the carcinoma penetrates the basement membrane, it is classified as invasive SCC with the ability to invade adjacent structures and metastasize.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Both CIS and invasive SCC can have keratin formation. The distinguishing factor is basement membrane integrity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CIS and invasive SCC have similar cytologic atypia. The key difference is invasion, not atypia degree.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tumor size criteria (3 cm) is used in adenocarcinoma staging (to classify frank adenocarcinoma), not as the CIS vs. invasive SCC distinction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lymph node metastasis occurs with invasive disease but is a consequence of invasion, not the defining criterion. Basement membrane penetration defines invasive disease.

questions.stem: Smoking increases the risk of NSCLC up to 20-fold. After quitting smoking, the NSCLC risk decreases but even after 30 years remains approximately which of the following compared to nonsmokers?

explanation.summary: After quitting smoking, NSCLC risk slowly decreases but never reaches a nonsmoker's level. By 30 years after quitting, the risk is still approximately double that of nonsmokers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Smoking increases NSCLC risk up to 20-fold. After quitting, the risk slowly decreases over time but never fully returns to a nonsmoker's baseline. Even 30 years after quitting, the NSCLC risk remains approximately double that of people who never smoked.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The risk never fully returns to nonsmoker levels. It remains elevated even decades after cessation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: By 30 years, the risk has decreased substantially from the 20-fold elevation during active smoking to approximately double.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ten times would be the approximate risk during or shortly after active smoking, not 30 years post-cessation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Twenty times is the peak risk during active smoking, not the residual risk 30 years after quitting.

questions.stem: The three stages of progressive cell transformation in lung adenocarcinoma are, in order, which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Squamous metaplasia â†’ dysplasia â†’ invasion

choices.label: B
choices.text: Atypical adenomatous hyperplasia â†’ adenocarcinoma in situ â†’ invasive adenocarcinoma

choices.label: C
choices.text: Carcinoma in situ â†’ hyperplasia â†’ invasion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bronchial metaplasia â†’ neuroendocrine differentiation â†’ invasion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Goblet cell hyperplasia â†’ mucin accumulation â†’ invasion

explanation.summary: Lung adenocarcinoma develops through: atypical adenomatous hyperplasia (small dysplastic pneumocyte foci) â†’ adenocarcinoma in situ (larger dysplastic areas) â†’ frank invasive adenocarcinoma (>3 cm with ability to invade and metastasize).
explanation.whyCorrect: The progressive cell transformations in adenocarcinoma are: (1) Atypical adenomatous hyperplasiaâ€”small areas of dysplastic pneumocytes; (2) Adenocarcinoma in situ (formerly bronchoalveolar carcinoma)â€”larger areas of dysplasia with increasingly atypical cytology; (3) Invasive adenocarcinomaâ€”lesion >3 cm with full ability to invade adjacent tissues and metastasize.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Squamous metaplasia â†’ dysplasia â†’ invasion describes the progression of squamous cell carcinoma, not adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This sequence is incorrectâ€”hyperplasia precedes CIS, not the reverse.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Neuroendocrine differentiation is the pathway for SCLC, not adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Goblet cell hyperplasia and mucin accumulation are not the standard progression stages of lung adenocarcinoma.

questions.stem: TP53 gene mutations are the most common genetic alteration in squamous cell carcinoma of the lung. On which chromosome is TP53 located?

explanation.summary: TP53 is located on chromosome 17 and is the most commonly mutated tumor suppressor gene in SCC of the lung. Other commonly affected genes include CDKN2 (chromosome 9) and RB1 (chromosome 13).
explanation.whyCorrect: TP53 gene mutations, located on chromosome 17, are the most common genetic alterations in squamous cell carcinoma of the lung. Other commonly affected tumor suppressor genes include CDKN2 on chromosome 9 and the retinoblastoma tumor suppressor gene RB1 on chromosome 13. These deletions/inactivations are induced by carcinogens in cigarette smoke.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chromosome 3 is a site of deletions in SCC but TP53 is specifically on chromosome 17.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Chromosome 9 is the location of CDKN2, not TP53.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Chromosome 13 is the location of RB1, not TP53.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chromosome 22 is not the location of TP53.

questions.stem: For small NSCLC tumors that are localized, the primary treatment with curative intent is which of the following?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Surgical resection followed by adjuvant therapy

explanation.summary: Small, localized NSCLC tumors (with only local lymph node extension) are treated with surgical resection followed by chemotherapy and/or targeted therapy. This offers the best chance of cure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Small tumors and those with only local extension to lymph nodes are treated with surgical resection, followed by chemotherapy and/or targeted therapy. This differs from SCLC, where surgery is rarely beneficial due to widespread metastasis. For NSCLC, surgery offers the best chance of cure when the cancer is detected early.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Chemotherapy alone is not curative for localized NSCLC. Surgery is the primary curative modality.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Radiation alone may be used for patients who cannot undergo surgery but is not the primary treatment for resectable tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Immunotherapy alone is not the standard primary treatment for localized NSCLC. It may be part of adjuvant therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Watchful waiting is inappropriate for confirmed NSCLC. Treatment should be initiated promptly.

questions.stem: Pembrolizumab has significantly improved outcomes for many NSCLC patients. Which of the following is its mechanism of action?

explanation.summary: Pembrolizumab is a PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitor that blocks the PD-1 receptor on T cells, preventing tumor-mediated immune evasion and allowing T cell-mediated tumor killing.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pembrolizumab is a PD-1 immune checkpoint inhibitor (blocking antibody). It blocks PD-1 on T cell surfaces, preventing tumor cells from evading immune detection through PD-L1 interaction. This reactivates T cell-mediated tumor killing. Pembrolizumab can be used regardless of specific PD-L1 expression status if other criteria are met. Common ADRs include fatigue, nausea, cough, decreased appetite, and skin rashes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: EGFR TKI inhibition is the mechanism of osimertinib, not pembrolizumab.
whyWrongByChoice.B: DNA cross-linking is the mechanism of cisplatin, not pembrolizumab.
whyWrongByChoice.D: ALK TKI inhibition is the mechanism of alectinib, not pembrolizumab.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Topoisomerase II inhibition is the mechanism of etoposide, not pembrolizumab.

questions.stem: Cisplatin is the most commonly used chemotherapy agent for lung and respiratory cancers. Which of the following best explains its preferential use for these cancers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is only active against squamous cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has affinity for lung cells and good lung tissue penetration

choices.label: C
choices.text: It has no side effects in lung cancer patients

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is the only available platinum agent

explanation.summary: Cisplatin is the most commonly used chemotherapy for lung cancers because it has affinity for lung cells and good penetration into lung tissue. Its side effects include ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity (the 'C's on chemoman').
explanation.whyCorrect: Cisplatin is the most commonly used chemotherapy for lung/respiratory cancers because it has an affinity for lung cells and good drug penetration into lung tissue. It is a platinum-based agent that causes direct DNA damage through intrastrand cross-linking. The most common side effects include ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cisplatin is active against many cell types, not only squamous cells. It is used across all lung cancer subtypes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cisplatin has significant side effects including ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Other platinum agents exist (carboplatin, oxaliplatin), but cisplatin is preferred for lung cancers due to its lung tissue affinity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cisplatin does not specifically target neuroendocrine cells. It causes DNA cross-linking in all rapidly dividing cells.

questions.stem: ALK-rearranged lung adenocarcinoma patients tend to have which of the following demographic characteristics?

explanation.summary: Patients with ALK-rearranged lung adenocarcinoma tend to be younger, light or nonsmokers. They are susceptible to specific ALK tyrosine kinase inhibitors like alectinib.
explanation.whyCorrect: ALK rearrangement in lung adenocarcinoma yields a new oncogene driving tumor growth. Patients with this rearrangement tend to be younger, light or nonsmokers, distinguishing them from the typical lung cancer patient population. They respond well to specific ALK tyrosine kinase inhibitors like alectinib.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Older heavy smokers are the typical demographic for all lung cancers, but ALK-rearranged patients specifically tend to be younger and lighter smokers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Elderly women with COPD are at risk for various lung cancers but are not the specific demographic for ALK-rearranged tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Asbestos workers are at risk for mesothelioma and lung cancer broadly, not specifically ALK-positive adenocarcinoma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Immunocompromised patients are at risk for opportunistic infections and some cancers but are not specifically associated with ALK-rearranged adenocarcinoma.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most common tumor suppressor gene mutation found in squamous cell carcinoma of the lung?

explanation.summary: TP53 gene mutations are the most common genetic alteration in squamous cell carcinoma, found on chromosome 17. TP53 encodes a tumor suppressor protein that regulates the cell cycle and prevents tumor formation.
explanation.whyCorrect: TP53 mutations are the most common genetic alterations in squamous cell carcinoma of the lung. The TP53 gene encodes tumor protein p53, a critical tumor suppressor that regulates the cell cycle. Its inactivation allows unregulated cell-cycle progression leading to cancer. Other commonly mutated tumor suppressor genes in SCC include CDKN2 and RB1.

whyWrongByChoice.A: EGFR is a gain-of-function mutation in adenocarcinoma, not a tumor suppressor in SCC.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ALK rearrangement is found in adenocarcinoma, not SCC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: KRAS is an oncogene mutation in adenocarcinoma (25%), not the most common TSG mutation in SCC.
whyWrongByChoice.E: MYC amplification is associated with SCLC, not SCC.

questions.stem: BRAF V600E mutations in adenocarcinoma are associated with which of the following clinical implications?

choices.label: B
choices.text: More aggressive disease and reduced survival

explanation.summary: BRAF V600E mutations in lung adenocarcinoma are associated with more aggressive disease and reduced survival. BRAF inhibitors can be used as targeted therapy for these patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: BRAF V600E mutations in adenocarcinoma are associated with a more aggressive disease course and reduced survival. However, these mutations are actionableâ€”BRAF inhibitors (dabrafenib) combined with MEK inhibitors (trametinib) can be used as targeted therapy. Knowing the BRAF status guides treatment decisions.

whyWrongByChoice.A: BRAF V600E mutations are associated with worse outcomes, not improved response to standard chemotherapy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: BRAF-mutant tumors do not spontaneously regress. They tend to be aggressive.
whyWrongByChoice.D: BRAF V600E mutations are associated with more aggressive disease, not lower metastatic risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: BRAF mutations are treated with BRAF inhibitors, not ALK inhibitors. These are different molecular targets.

questions.stem: When lung adenocarcinoma is diagnosed, molecular/genetic testing is needed. Which of the following best explains why this testing is critical?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To determine if the tumor is primary or metastatic

choices.label: B
choices.text: To identify mutations susceptible to targeted therapy

choices.label: C
choices.text: To confirm the diagnosis on imaging

choices.label: D
choices.text: To replace the need for biopsy

explanation.summary: Molecular testing of adenocarcinoma identifies mutations (EGFR, ALK, BRAF, KRAS, ROS1, MET) that guide targeted therapy selection. This 'precision medicine' approach can offer better outcomes than conventional chemotherapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: When adenocarcinoma is diagnosed, molecular/genetic testing is needed to identify mutations susceptible to targeted therapy (e.g., EGFR â†’ osimertinib, ALK â†’ alectinib, BRAF â†’ BRAF inhibitors). This 'precision medicine' approach matches treatment to the specific molecular driver of the patient's cancer, often providing better outcomes than conventional chemotherapy alone.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Distinguishing primary from metastatic cancer uses immunohistochemistry (TTF-1, etc.), not the molecular testing for therapeutic targets.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Imaging identifies the tumor but molecular testing guides treatment, not imaging confirmation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Molecular testing supplements biopsyâ€”it does not replace the need for tissue diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Smoking status is determined by clinical history, not molecular testing.
